unit.name: Compounding Control & Recipe Management System (MES/SCADA)
unit.cfj: Execute, record, and enforce compounding recipes and process parameters

meta.method: Ulwick ODI (Importance × Satisfaction → Opportunity), needs scoring v2

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that contamination is introduced during a compounding step
rows.plain: Reduce the chance of bringing germs into the product while combining its ingredients.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination during a compounding step is somewhat adjacent to recipe execution (relevance 8) but can cause patient death, the worst outcome (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Contamination control depends on procedures and gowning rather than system enforcement, so residual risk remains chronically high. Contamination often only becomes evident at post-hoc testing rather than in real time during the step. Preventing contamination relies heavily on operator aseptic technique and discipline that tools cannot enforce. A contaminated batch is a total loss, and current controls only partially prevent it.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an incorrect ingredient is charged into the batch
rows.plain: Prevent the wrong ingredient from being added to the medicine. Using the wrong ingredient could make the product unsafe or ineffective.
rows.imp_rat: Charging a wrong ingredient directly defeats the CFJ of executing correct recipes (relevance 10) and can produce toxic or fatal product (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Mature MES barcode-gated charging substantially reduces wrong-ingredient events, though manual overrides and non-serialized items leave residual risk. Barcode/weigh-scan verification at charging happens in-line and adds little delay to the operator's step sequence. Electronic material verification offloads the burden of manual ingredient identification onto the MES, reducing reliance on operator vigilance. Wrong-ingredient batches are costly to scrap, so the verification investment is generally justified and widely deployed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that compounding resumes with an incorrect recipe revision or parameter set after a disruption
rows.plain: After a production problem, the process should not restart with the wrong instructions or settings. This helps prevent the product from being made incorrectly.
rows.imp_rat: Resuming with wrong recipe revision after disruption is a real enforcement gap (relevance 8) that can ruin a batch already partly processed (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Standard MES does version the recipe, but disruption recovery is precisely where checkpoint state and human handoff break down, leaving a real recurring failure mode. Restart after a disruption often stalls while the overseer manually confirms the active recipe revision across MES screens and paper batch records. Correct resumption leans heavily on an experienced overseer remembering exactly where the batch was and which revision governs it, a fragile point of judgement. Wrong-revision restarts can scrap an entire compounded lot, so the financial exposure of this need is high.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterile-boundary breaches at product-contact interfaces during compounding
rows.plain: Prevent breaks in the barriers that keep the product germ-free where the equipment touches it during mixing. This helps keep germs out of the product.
rows.imp_rat: Sterile-boundary breaches at product-contact interfaces are contamination events tangential to recipe logic (relevance 7) but potentially lethal (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: MES/SCADA has little visibility into physical sterile-boundary breaches, leaving this need largely dependent on manual practice. Sterile-boundary breaches are typically detected only after the fact, not prevented during the interface event. Aseptic connection integrity relies almost entirely on manual operator technique at product-contact points. Breaches force batch rejection, an expense that current practice poorly averts.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of material with compromised sterile status entering the compounding path
rows.plain: Prevent materials whose germ-free condition may have been compromised from entering the mixing process. This helps keep the product clean and safe.
rows.imp_rat: Compromised-sterile material entering the path is upstream contamination control (relevance 7) with catastrophic patient-safety stakes (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Status verification is procedural and record-based, so compromised-sterility material can still enter the path when records are incomplete. Sterility status of materials is verified through separate QA workflows that can lag the compounding start. Judging compromised sterile status depends on human review of records rather than automated gating. Using compromised material scraps the batch, a cost only partly controlled by status checks.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of compounding beginning with unconfirmed product-contact-path sterility
rows.plain: Do not begin mixing or preparing the product until it is confirmed that every product-contact pathway is germ-free. This prevents contamination from starting at the beginning of the work.
rows.imp_rat: Beginning with unconfirmed path sterility is a contamination-control precondition adjacent to execution (relevance 7) but can cause fatal contamination (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Sterility confirmation is largely a checklist/procedural step, so unconfirmed starts still occur without hard system interlocks. Confirming path sterility before start relies on manual sign-offs that can delay or be shortcut. Operators must interpret sterilization records rather than rely on an automated readiness gate. Starting with unconfirmed sterility risks total batch loss, poorly guarded today.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that compounding proceeds with process parameters outside approved limits
rows.plain: Prevent mixing or preparation from continuing when the process settings are outside the approved limits, so the product is made according to required rules.
rows.imp_rat: Enforcing approved parameter limits is the essence of the scope's job (relevance 10); running outside limits yields out-of-spec or unsafe batches (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Electronic recipe enforcement with hard/soft limits is mature and widely deployed, giving high consistency though gaps remain for manual steps and unmodeled parameters. Modern MES/SCADA enforces parameter limits in real time so out-of-range conditions are flagged or blocked without delay. Automated interlocks reduce reliance on operator vigilance, though correctly configured limit sets still require skilled recipe engineers. Preventing out-of-limit batches avoids scrapped material and rework, delivering strong cost avoidance where enforcement is configured.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that combined parameter drift remains undetected while individual readings remain within approved limits
rows.plain: Detect when several process settings drift together even if each separate reading still appears acceptable, so a developing problem is not missed.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected combined drift while individual readings pass limits is a subtle enforcement gap (relevance 7) that can silently produce nonconforming product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Single-parameter limit checking is the norm and multivariate monitoring is largely absent, leaving this need essentially unserved. Detecting combined drift requires cross-parameter analysis that no one performs in real time during a shift. Recognizing interacting drift while each reading stays in-limit demands advanced multivariate insight almost no overseer applies manually. Undetected combined drift can quietly ruin batches, an expensive latent failure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of residual contamination remaining in the product-contact path from prior production
rows.plain: Make it less likely that leftover contamination from earlier production remains in the path that touches the product. This helps keep the next product safe and germ-free.
rows.imp_rat: Residual contamination from prior production is a cleaning/changeover concern near execution (relevance 7) with potentially lethal cross-contamination (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning validation and status tracking help, but confirmation of residual removal is often manual and not system-enforced per run. Cleaning verification is typically a batch-preparation activity, not confirmed in real time at run start. Residual-contamination assurance depends on cleaning validation and visual inspection skill. Cross-contamination causes rejection, a loss only partly mitigated by cleaning programs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of compromising sterile product integrity during disruption recovery
rows.plain: Make it less likely that sterile products become contaminated while work is being restored after a disruption. This helps protect the product when normal operations are interrupted.
rows.imp_rat: Compromising sterile integrity during disruption recovery is contamination control in a recovery scenario (relevance 6) with catastrophic potential (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: SOPs and aseptic training help, but disruption recovery introduces non-routine interventions where sterility assurance is genuinely fragile. Sterile recovery steps are slow and any delay increases contamination exposure, working against the overseer. Preserving sterility during recovery demands high aseptic expertise and strict technique that not every shift member reliably applies. A sterility breach destroys the batch and can idle a suite, an extremely costly outcome.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recipe steps executed with aseptic parameters outside approved limits
rows.plain: Reduce how often recipe steps are carried out while sterile-processing settings are outside their approved limits. This matters to keep the process within safe and reliable conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Aseptic parameters out of limits during steps sits between recipe enforcement and contamination control (relevance 7); breach can be fatal (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Environmental monitoring exists but is not always tightly linked to recipe-step gating, leaving recurring out-of-limit execution. Aseptic parameters are monitored continuously so out-of-limit steps can be flagged during execution. Operators still interpret many aseptic conditions rather than relying on hard automated limits. Steps run outside aseptic limits jeopardize the batch, a cost partially contained by monitoring.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that material with an out-of-specification strength, concentration, grade, or purity is accepted
rows.plain: Avoid accepting material whose strength, amount, type, or cleanliness is outside the required limits. Using material that meets those limits helps ensure the product is made correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting out-of-spec strength/purity material feeds bad input to compounding (relevance 8) and can produce lethal potency errors (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Established incoming-QC and release workflows reliably catch out-of-spec strength or purity before use. CoA review and QA release occur before dispensing, adding upfront time but not per-step delay. Specification conformance is checked against documented limits, reducing operator judgement burden. Accepting off-spec material wastes an entire batch, and release gating largely prevents that loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that material with an incorrect identity is accepted as conforming
rows.plain: Prevent materials from being accepted when they are not the materials they are supposed to be. This helps ensure the correct materials are used in production.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting wrong-identity material as conforming corrupts inputs to the recipe (relevance 8) and can be fatal (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Incoming identity testing plus dispensing scans form a mature, reliable double-check against misidentification. Identity verification via label/barcode at receipt and dispensing is fast and routine. Barcode and ID testing reduce reliance on operator recognition of correct identity. Wrong-identity material causes total batch failure, well guarded by ID testing and scanning.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that out-of-bound process parameters are allowed to proceed during compounding
rows.plain: Prevent the mixing process from continuing when important settings are outside the allowed limits. This helps keep the product consistent and prevents problems caused by incorrect processing.
rows.imp_rat: Allowing out-of-bound parameters to proceed is a direct enforcement failure (relevance 8) that can yield contaminated or unsafe product (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Established systems routinely block or flag out-of-bound parameters, though configuration gaps mean the protection is not absolute. Mature MES/SCADA platforms enforce parameter bounds in real time, so no evaluation-time delay is added once configured. Configuring bound enforcement still requires control expertise, but the underlying capability is standard and well understood. Preventing out-of-bound proceeding avoids costly rejected batches, a well-recognized payoff.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of personnel interventions that expose the aseptic zone during compounding
rows.plain: Reduce how often people need to reach into or otherwise disturb the sterile work area during mixing. This lowers the chance of bringing in contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Personnel interventions exposing the aseptic zone are contamination-control acts loosely tied to recipe execution (relevance 6) but a leading cause of fatal contamination (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Personnel interventions remain a leading contamination source with little system-level prevention beyond procedures. Interventions occur in real time and current systems do little to reduce their duration. Minimizing zone-exposing interventions depends on operator technique and process design, not tooling. Each intervention raises contamination risk and potential batch loss, poorly mitigated today.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an unauthorized recipe or parameter change is released to production
rows.plain: Prevent unapproved changes to the recipe or machine settings from being used in production. This helps keep production within approved requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Unauthorized recipe/parameter change released to production undermines enforcement (relevance 9) and can propagate defective batches at scale (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Version-controlled recipe management with enforced approval gates is a mature standard practice, though shadow parameters and privileged overrides remain residual risks. Electronic change-control workflows route approvals promptly but can still stall pending required signatories. Role-based access and e-signatures reduce dependence on individual diligence to catch unauthorized changes. Blocking unauthorized releases avoids costly recalls and rejected batches, a clear resource saving.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of recommending acceptance while material compliance gaps remain
rows.plain: Do not recommend accepting something while required rules or material requirements are still unmet. This helps prevent materials from being approved before they are fully suitable.
rows.imp_rat: Recommending acceptance with compliance gaps is a release-decision error somewhat downstream of execution (relevance 7) but can release harmful product to patients (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Checklists and dual sign-off catch many gaps but residual compliance gaps still slip through because coverage depends on human diligence. Reviewers spend considerable time cross-checking records before signing off, so a clean recommendation is not fast under today's practice. Avoiding acceptance while gaps remain relies heavily on experienced reviewers manually spotting what automated checks miss. A wrongly cleared batch carries enormous recall, remediation and regulatory-exposure costs that the status quo only partly guards against.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that affected compounded material advances to filling before disruption-related quality impact is established
rows.plain: Stop material affected by a production problem from reaching the filling step before its quality has been checked. This prevents unsuitable material from being put into containers.
rows.imp_rat: Advancing affected material to filling before impact assessment is a disruption-handling gap (relevance 6) that can release harmful product downstream (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Status-quo holds rely on manual quarantine flags and human discipline under time pressure, so affected material advancing prematurely remains a genuine recurring risk. Holding material until quality impact is established adds delay that pressures the overseer to release before assessment is complete. Deciding whether disruption-affected material is safe demands scarce quality judgement that the overseer often lacks in the moment. Advancing compromised material to filling wastes fill components and can force downstream recalls, a severe cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between recorded parameter values and actual compounding conditions
rows.plain: Keep the recorded settings as close as possible to the conditions that actually occurred while ingredients were being mixed and prepared. This helps make the records trustworthy and reveals real process problems.
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between record and actual conditions defeats the recording obligation (relevance 9) and is a serious data-integrity finding (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Direct instrument capture is dependable, but conditions read manually or inferred still drift from what gets recorded. Real-time SCADA polling narrows the window between actual conditions and their record for automated tags. Judging whether a recorded value truly reflects conditions still leans on operator familiarity where sensing is indirect. Existing process instrumentation covers most conditions without new spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an unauthorized recipe version governs batch execution
rows.plain: Make sure workers use only the approved version of the production instructions for the batch. This prevents an outdated or unauthorized set of instructions from controlling the work.
rows.imp_rat: An unauthorized version governing execution is a direct enforcement breach (relevance 9) that can systematically produce unsafe batches (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Modern MES/SCADA enforce approved-version execution effectively, making unauthorized-version execution rare. Version control checks are largely automated, so confirming the governing version is quick. System-managed version locking reduces the expertise needed to ensure the right recipe runs. Preventing unauthorized versions avoids costly batch losses and investigations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between recorded parameter values and values actually observed during compounding
rows.plain: Keep recorded measurements as close as possible to what was actually seen while the mixture was being made. This helps ensure the production record can be trusted.
rows.imp_rat: Recorded values diverging from observed values corrupts the recording pillar of the CFJ (relevance 9) and creates data-integrity/regulatory exposure (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Where instruments feed the MES directly, recorded-versus-observed fidelity is high, but manual-entry steps remain a recurring source of small deviations. Automated data acquisition captures many parameters at observation time, though manual readings still lag and introduce transcription gaps. Operators must still notice and correctly transcribe values where automation is absent, demanding attention that varies by shift. Sensor and MES infrastructure exists in most modern facilities, so incremental cost per record is low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of treating a transient excursion as a stable recipe endpoint
rows.plain: Lower the chance that a brief, temporary change is mistaken for a steady sign that the recipe is finished. This helps prevent the process from stopping too early or at the wrong time.
rows.imp_rat: Treating a transient excursion as endpoint mis-terminates the recipe (relevance 8) leaving product under-processed (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Stability-confirmation logic (dwell timers, trend checks) is inconsistently implemented, so transient excursions are still frequently mistaken for endpoints. Distinguishing transient from stable requires a settling/dwell period that current systems handle inconsistently. Recognizing a false-stable endpoint demands experienced operator judgement that is not well supported by tools. Acting on a transient endpoint wastes material and forces rework, a cost poorly mitigated today.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an unreleased or rejected material lot is accepted for use
rows.plain: Prevent materials that have not been approved or have been rejected from being used. This helps ensure only acceptable materials enter the process.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting an unreleased/rejected lot puts unqualified material into the batch (relevance 8) with severe patient-safety and compliance consequences (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Quarantine/release status enforcement in inventory and MES reliably blocks unreleased or rejected lots from use. Release-status checks are electronic and instantaneous at dispensing where inventory is integrated. System status flags remove the operator's need to judge lot release manually. Using rejected lots would scrap batches, and status locking prevents that cost effectively.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of critical parameter excursions beyond specified limits during production
rows.plain: Keep important production settings within their allowed limits. This helps the process produce safe and consistent results.
rows.imp_rat: Critical parameter excursions are exactly what the system must prevent (relevance 9); frequent excursions degrade quality and safety (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: SCADA control loops and alarms reduce excursion frequency well for automated parameters but manual-controlled steps still drift. Continuous SCADA monitoring flags excursions promptly so corrective action can begin quickly. Automated alarms reduce the operator vigilance needed to spot parameter drift. Excursions risk batch loss, and closed-loop control curbs some but not all of that expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that material with contamination or container compromise is accepted for use
rows.plain: Do not use material if it may be contaminated or if its container may be damaged. Material should be accepted only when it is in suitable condition.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting contaminated or container-compromised material introduces contamination into compounding (relevance 7) with potentially lethal outcome (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Receiving inspection and quarantine catch most compromised containers, though subtle contamination can pass visual checks. Container inspection is a quick receiving step but relies on manual observation timing. Detecting container compromise or contamination depends on inspector visual skill. Accepting compromised material risks batch loss, mostly averted by receiving inspection.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required recipe steps omitted during a compounding batch
rows.plain: Make sure no required instruction is skipped while preparing a batch. Completing every step helps the batch turn out as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required steps during a batch is a direct recipe-execution failure (relevance 9) leaving product incomplete or unsafe (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Order enforcement makes omitted steps rare, though manual or off-system steps can still be skipped. Enforced sequencing prevents skipping steps that would otherwise be caught only late. Interlocked workflows reduce the skill needed to keep track of the full step list. Step interlocks are configured within the existing recipe framework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that material affected by an unresolved storage excursion is accepted for use
rows.plain: Make sure material is not used when it may have been affected by a storage problem that has not yet been resolved. This helps prevent unsuitable material from entering production.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting material under unresolved storage excursion introduces possibly degraded input (relevance 7) with serious quality/safety risk (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Storage-excursion tracking is less integrated than release status, so affected lots can slip through when holds are not flagged in the system. Storage-excursion holds depend on QA processes that may not be resolved before material is needed. Recognizing an unresolved storage excursion requires linking monitoring data to the lot, often a manual review. Using excursion-affected material risks batch loss, only partly controlled by hold status.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of recommending acceptance while a critical recipe-control or compliance requirement remains unproven
rows.plain: Do not recommend accepting a batch while an important production or compliance requirement still lacks proof. Acceptance should wait until the requirement has been demonstrated.
rows.imp_rat: Recommending acceptance with an unproven critical control is a release-decision defect (relevance 6) that can release unsafe or contaminated product (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Traceability matrices and acceptance checklists exist but frequently let unproven items slip through under schedule pressure, so this remains a recurring risk. Assembling the full body of proof before signing off is slow, and reviewers often face pressure to recommend before every requirement is closed. Judging whether a critical requirement is truly proven demands seasoned validation and GMP expertise that is scarce and unevenly distributed across review teams. The cost of a premature acceptance is high, but there is little dedicated budget for the deep independent scrutiny needed to prevent it.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of evaluating a batch against limits from the wrong approved recipe revision
rows.plain: Avoid judging a production batch using limits from the wrong approved version of its instructions. Using the correct version helps ensure the batch is judged by the right requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Evaluating against the wrong revision's limits corrupts enforcement judgment (relevance 8) and can pass a truly nonconforming batch (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Approved-revision locking in modern MES makes wrong-revision evaluation fairly rare in steady state, though edge cases and revision changeovers still slip through. Evaluating against approved limits is usually near-instant once the correct revision is loaded in the system. The system carries the limits, so the operator does not need to recall them, but confirming the right revision still requires attention. Judging a batch against wrong limits risks releasing bad product or scrapping good product, a meaningful cost either way.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of production batches whose executed parameters conflict with the approved recipe
rows.plain: Make sure the settings actually used during production match the approved instructions. This helps ensure every batch is made the way it was authorized.
rows.imp_rat: Executed parameters conflicting with approved recipe is a direct enforcement failure (relevance 9) yielding nonconforming batches (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: When recipes are managed electronically the executed parameters closely track the approved master, but manual and offline steps still introduce conflicts. Executed-vs-approved comparison happens automatically during batch execution so conflicts surface in real time. Automated recipe download reduces the skill needed to reproduce parameters correctly compared with manual transcription. Preventing parameter conflicts reduces scrapped batches and investigation costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of declaring a recipe endpoint before required conditions are satisfied
rows.plain: Do not say the process is finished until all required conditions have been met. This helps ensure the product receives the full treatment.
rows.imp_rat: Declaring endpoint prematurely means the recipe was not fully executed (relevance 8), producing under-processed product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Automated endpoint gating exists but many endpoints still depend on operator declaration, leaving frequent premature-call risk. Endpoint confirmation logic runs continuously without adding operator wait beyond required measurement time. Endpoint judgement still leans heavily on operator interpretation when criteria are not fully automated. Premature endpoints cause reprocessing or scrap, a recurring cost only partially contained.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of confirming a recipe step before all required actions and checks are complete
rows.plain: Do not mark a process step complete until every required action and check is finished. This prevents the work from moving ahead too soon.
rows.imp_rat: Confirming a step before actions/checks complete is a false-execution enforcement failure (relevance 8) that can advance an incomplete unsafe batch (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Where checks are interlocked confirmation is reliable, but many completeness judgements still rest on the operator. Interlocks can block premature confirmation without adding operator delay. Enforced checklists reduce reliance on operator judgement about completeness. Configuring completion interlocks is a design-time engineering cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of parameter-limit overrides without required authorization or documented rationale
rows.plain: Prevent people from changing required process settings without approval or a written explanation. This helps keep production controlled and properly documented.
rows.imp_rat: Unauthorized limit overrides subvert enforcement (relevance 8) and can permit unsafe conditions plus regulatory findings (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Systems can require authorization and rationale for overrides, but weak reason-code discipline and privileged bypasses leave real recurring gaps. Override capture at the point of action is quick when the system prompts for it. Enforced reason-code and authorization prompts reduce reliance on operators remembering to document rationale. Undocumented overrides drive costly investigations and potential batch rejection.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount by which an ingredient charge deviates from the recipe quantity
rows.plain: Add as close as possible to the amount of ingredient called for in the recipe. This helps the finished product have the intended composition.
rows.imp_rat: Quantity deviation from recipe is central to accurate compounding (relevance 9); large deviations affect strength/potency but are graded rather than binary catastrophic (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Interfaced scales with recipe tolerance gating catch most quantity deviations, but manual transcription and coarse manual dispensing still allow drift. Tolerance-checked weighing gives immediate accept/reject feedback without slowing the charge. Automated tolerance bands relieve the operator from judging whether a weight is close enough by eye. Over/under charges waste material and cause rework, and scale integration modestly mitigates that expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which indicated critical parameter values differ from actual conditions
rows.plain: Make sure the displayed readings for important process conditions match what is really happening. This allows workers to control the process based on trustworthy information.
rows.imp_rat: Indicated critical values differing from actual conditions corrupts the enforcement signal (relevance 8) and can allow undetected unsafe processing (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Calibration programs bound indicated-vs-actual divergence, but between calibrations undetected drift remains possible. Sensor readings display in real time so no delay in seeing indicated values. Detecting a sensor drift versus true value requires calibration knowledge beyond routine operation. Acting on wrong indicated values can waste batches, mitigated only by periodic calibration.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between the recorded quantity and the quantity actually added
rows.plain: Make sure the amount recorded is the same as the amount actually added. This keeps the production record accurate and prevents mistakes in the recipe.
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between recorded and actual quantity added corrupts the record (relevance 8) and misrepresents potency, a serious defect (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Scale-to-MES links reduce quantity discrepancies substantially, yet manual overrides and partial additions still cause mismatches. Weigh-scale integration can post quantities immediately, but manual dispense confirmation adds delay. Correctly reconciling target versus actual dispensed amount depends on operator care during weighing. Integrated scales are common and add little marginal cost once installed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required recipe steps omitted during batch execution
rows.plain: Leave out fewer required steps when following the instructions for making a production run. This helps ensure the product is made completely and correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required steps directly breaks recipe execution (relevance 9); missing a step can leave product unsafe or ineffective (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: EBR step-completion gating reliably prevents skipping mandatory steps, though non-enforced manual notes remain a gap. Forced-step acknowledgement adds minor prompting time but prevents costly restarts. Mandatory electronic sign-offs remove the operator's need to remember every required step. Omitted steps causing batch loss are expensive, and step gating avoids much of that waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of executed batches containing compounding parameters that differ from the approved recipe
rows.plain: Have fewer production runs in which the mixing settings differ from the approved instructions. This helps ensure the product is made as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Batches with parameters differing from approved recipe is the definitional enforcement failure (relevance 9) yielding nonconforming product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: MES parameter enforcement and automatic deviation flagging make this one of the better-served needs today. Comparing executed batch parameters against the approved recipe is increasingly automated so deviations surface quickly. Enforced recipes reduce reliance on operator skill, though interpreting flagged differences still needs review. Automated enforcement prevents costly off-recipe batches before they complete, saving material and rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of starting a recipe step with equipment outside the required state
rows.plain: Do not start a recipe step until the equipment is in the required condition. This helps prevent errors and unsafe or poor-quality results.
rows.imp_rat: Starting a step with equipment outside required state violates enforced preconditions (relevance 8) and can damage the batch or equipment (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Equipment interlocks and state checks exist for major utilities but coverage is uneven, leaving gaps for un-instrumented conditions. Equipment-state checks at step start can be automated but often still require manual confirmation, adding minor delay. Interlocks reduce the operator's burden of verifying equipment state before proceeding. Starting in a wrong equipment state can spoil the step, a cost partly avoided by interlocks.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recorded parameter values differing from the values actually observed
rows.plain: Have fewer recorded settings that differ from what was actually seen during production. This keeps the production record accurate.
rows.imp_rat: Recorded values differing from observed values undermines the recording job (relevance 8) and is a data-integrity violation (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Differing recorded values are markedly reduced by automation but persist wherever human keying remains. Automated capture avoids the delay of periodic manual logging that lets errors accumulate. Fewer manual entries mean less reliance on operator transcription accuracy. The recording infrastructure is largely sunk cost in existing systems.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that reported quality indicators misrepresent recorded recipe execution or process parameters
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that reported quality results give a misleading picture of the instructions followed or the settings used during production. This helps people make decisions using an accurate view of performance.
rows.imp_rat: Quality indicators misrepresenting recorded execution corrupts the trustworthiness of records (relevance 7) and can drive wrong release decisions (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Direct system-sourced indicators reduce misrepresentation, but manual calculations, spreadsheets and mapping errors still distort reported values. Automated derivation of indicators from source records is fast once configured. Validating that indicator logic faithfully reflects execution requires analytical skill. Misrepresentative indicators cause misdirected costs and regulatory exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an obsolete recipe version is applied during compounding
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that outdated mixing instructions are used to prepare a product. This helps ensure the product is made according to the current requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Applying an obsolete recipe version during compounding violates enforcement (relevance 8) and yields product against outdated, possibly unsafe specs (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Approved-version enforcement is a standard MES feature that reliably prevents obsolete recipes where properly configured, so this need is largely met. Version control in modern recipe-management systems applies the correct version automatically without manual delay. Recipe versioning is a mature, well-documented capability requiring modest configuration skill. Preventing obsolete-recipe use avoids expensive scrap and reprocessing.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that the production environment remains outside aseptic limits during compounding
rows.plain: Spend less time with the production area not meeting the required germ-free conditions while the ingredients are being mixed. This lowers the chance that the product becomes contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Environment out of aseptic limits during compounding is environmental control adjacent to the scope (relevance 6) with severe contamination consequences (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Environmental monitoring detects the excursion but does not shorten it, so duration outside limits remains variable. Recovery time to aseptic limits after a disturbance is largely uncontrolled and monitored only passively. Bringing the environment back within limits depends on operator and HVAC response judgement. Prolonged out-of-limit environment threatens the batch, an expense weakly controlled.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of nonconforming preparations not signaled by monitored quality indicators
rows.plain: Have fewer preparations that fail to meet requirements without being flagged by the quality checks being watched. This helps identify problems before they go unnoticed.
rows.imp_rat: Nonconforming preparations not signaled by monitored indicators is a detection-reliability failure (relevance 6) that can let harmful product pass (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Indicator coverage is incomplete and many quality failure modes are not captured by monitored parameters, so escapes remain common. Monitored indicators update near real time but detection of nonconformance can lag actual quality failure. Deciding which indicators truly signal nonconformance requires substantial quality expertise not fully automated. Missed signals allow nonconforming product to advance, incurring downstream and recall costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of executing recipe steps outside the approved sequence
rows.plain: Lower the chance that recipe instructions are carried out in the wrong order. This helps ensure the process follows the approved method.
rows.imp_rat: Out-of-sequence step execution violates recipe enforcement (relevance 9) and can cause reactions or contamination, though many sequences are recoverable (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Electronic phase/sequence enforcement is a mature MES capability that reliably blocks out-of-sequence execution where deployed. Enforced step sequencing prevents out-of-order actions instantly rather than requiring after-the-fact rework time. The MES electronic batch record enforces the workflow so operators need not memorize the correct order. Sequence violations that ruin batches are expensive, and enforcement avoids those losses at modest system cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a consequential excursion is overlooked among nonactionable indications during the shift
rows.plain: Lower the chance that an important process problem is missed among alerts that do not require action during a work shift. This helps ensure serious problems are noticed and handled.
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking a consequential excursion amid noise is an alarm-management concern (relevance 6); a missed critical excursion can produce unsafe product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Poorly rationalized alarm systems mean genuine excursions are routinely buried, so this need is served badly today. Sifting consequential excursions from alarm noise consumes attention the overseer cannot spare in real time. Distinguishing an actionable excursion amid nuisance alarms demands experienced judgement and constant vigilance, the classic alarm-flood problem. An overlooked excursion can spoil a batch, so the cost of a miss is high.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that material beyond its expiration or retest date is accepted for use
rows.plain: Do not accept materials that are past their expiration date or their required review date. This helps ensure only approved materials are used.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting expired/beyond-retest material feeds degraded input (relevance 8) risking sub-potency or safety issues (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Expiry and retest-date gating in inventory systems is mature and reliably blocks expired material. Expiry/retest dates are checked automatically at dispensing without operator delay. System date-checking removes reliance on the operator reading and comparing dates. Expired-material batch loss is well prevented by automated date enforcement.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of exceeding the allowable aseptic hold interval between recipe steps
rows.plain: Keep the time between production steps within the allowed limit while protecting the product from germs. This helps maintain the product's cleanliness during processing.
rows.imp_rat: Exceeding aseptic hold interval between steps is a contamination-timing constraint tied to sequencing (relevance 6) with severe sterility consequences (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Hold-timer enforcement is inconsistently automated, so exceeding aseptic hold limits still occurs. Aseptic hold intervals are tracked but timers are often manual and easy to overrun. Tracking allowable hold windows depends on operator awareness of the limits. Exceeding hold intervals can force batch rejection, a cost weakly guarded.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material risks in recorded or enforced compounding recipes and parameters that remain undetected
rows.plain: Make sure important risks in written or enforced mixing instructions and settings are found before approval. This helps prevent unsafe or incorrect preparation.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected material risks in recorded recipes is a detection concern (relevance 6) whose escape can lead to serious quality escapes (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Detection is inconsistent because it hinges on individual judgement rather than systematic risk enumeration. Risk detection in recipes and parameters is a slow analytical task with no quick shortcut today. Spotting latent material risks depends almost entirely on the expertise and imagination of senior reviewers. Undetected risks that surface later are far costlier than the review effort, which itself is expert-intensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical parameters assigned limits that conflict with the approved recipe
rows.plain: Reduce the number of important process settings whose allowed limits disagree with the approved production instructions. Matching limits help workers run the process correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Critical parameters assigned limits conflicting with the approved recipe corrupt the enforcement baseline (relevance 8) so batches pass against wrong bounds (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Version-controlled recipe parameters keep limits aligned, though manual SCADA overrides can still diverge. Recipe-defined limits load automatically without configuration delay at runtime. Approved-recipe limit management removes operator responsibility for setting limits. Conflicting limits can misjudge quality, mostly prevented by controlled recipes.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time a critical parameter remains outside specified limits before correction takes effect
rows.plain: Shorten the time that an important process setting stays outside its allowed range before it is brought back. Quick correction helps keep the process under control.
rows.imp_rat: Time a critical parameter stays out of limits before correction directly affects enforcement effectiveness (relevance 8); prolonged excursion damages product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Correction latency depends on control-loop tuning and operator response, giving good but variable performance. Alarms and closed-loop control shorten the interval between excursion and correction considerably. Automated control response reduces dependence on the operator reacting fast enough. Shorter out-of-limit time limits material degradation and scrap cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of recorded process data lost during compounding interruptions
rows.plain: Avoid losing the information recorded about the process when mixing is paused or interrupted. This keeps the production record complete and trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Recorded data lost during interruptions creates record gaps (relevance 7) that can force batch rejection and cause compliance issues (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Buffering and redundancy protect most data, but control interruptions still risk losing un-committed process data. Recovery routines aim to restore data quickly after an interruption. Restoration depends on IT support rather than operator skill during outages. Redundant storage and UPS add real infrastructure cost that not all sites fully implement.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the batch record identifies a recipe revision other than the one executed
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that the production record names a different recipe version from the one actually used. This matters because the record must accurately show how the product was made.
rows.imp_rat: Batch record citing wrong revision is a core recording/traceability defect (relevance 8) with serious data-integrity consequences (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Where an EBR is in place the executed revision is bound to the record reliably; residual risk lives in manual or hybrid paper systems. Electronic batch records auto-stamp the executed revision, so recording it costs essentially no time. Automatic capture removes reliance on the operator to transcribe the correct revision identifier. A record naming the wrong revision triggers costly investigations, but automation keeps this uncommon.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a critical departure is overlooked amid simultaneous deviation indications
rows.plain: An important problem should not be missed when several warning signs appear at the same time. Finding it promptly helps prevent a larger problem.
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking a critical departure amid simultaneous deviations is an alarm/detection concern (relevance 6); a missed critical departure can yield unsafe product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Alarm-flood management and prioritization are weak in many systems, so critical departures are frequently overlooked. Simultaneous alarms appear at once, but discerning the critical one among them consumes reaction time. Prioritizing a critical departure amid alarm floods depends heavily on operator experience and attention. Missing the critical one amid noise can lose a batch, poorly mitigated today.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of applying endpoint criteria from the wrong recipe version to the active batch
rows.plain: Reduce the chance of using the end-point rules from an outdated recipe for the current batch. This helps ensure the batch is judged using the correct instructions.
rows.imp_rat: Applying endpoint criteria from the wrong recipe version to the active batch is a direct enforcement error (relevance 8) that can mis-terminate and produce nonconforming product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: MES recipe version control reliably ties endpoint criteria to the active approved recipe. Version-controlled recipes load instantly without operator lookup time. Recipe version management removes the operator's burden of picking the right version. Wrong-version endpoints scrap batches, well prevented by version control.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between recorded parameter values and actual compounding conditions
rows.plain: Make recorded settings match the conditions that actually existed while ingredients were being combined. This helps reveal when the process did not run as expected.
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between recorded values and actual conditions defeats the recording obligation (relevance 8) and is a serious data-integrity finding (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: SCADA-recorded values closely track actual conditions where instruments are calibrated, so recorded-versus-actual discrepancy is fairly well controlled. Automated sensors log values continuously, so there is no delay introducing discrepancy from manual timing. Direct instrument capture removes the transcription skill that used to introduce discrepancies. Discrepancies trigger investigations, but automated capture keeps these costs contained.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of recorded parameter values that differ from values applied or observed during compounding
rows.plain: Make sure recorded process settings match what was actually used or observed while the material was being mixed. This keeps the production record accurate.
rows.imp_rat: Recorded values differing from applied/observed values is a core recording defect (relevance 8) with data-integrity consequences (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Electronically captured readings rarely differ from applied values once instruments feed the record directly, making this well served. Direct electronic capture eliminates the lag that manual re-entry once introduced. Automatic data acquisition removes dependence on careful manual transcription. Fewer transcription errors mean fewer costly record reconciliations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the selected recipe version does not match the approved batch order
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the wrong recipe is used for an approved production order. Matching the recipe to the order helps ensure the batch is made as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Selected recipe not matching the approved batch order is a selection/enforcement error (relevance 8) that produces the wrong product against orders (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: MES order-recipe linkage reliably ensures the executed version matches the approved batch order. Batch-order-to-recipe binding is automatic with no lookup delay. Order-recipe matching is system-enforced, not left to operator selection. Running the wrong recipe scraps a batch, well prevented by order binding.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of parameter entries containing an incorrect unit, sign, or decimal placement
rows.plain: Reduce entries with the wrong measurement unit, plus or minus sign, or decimal point. Correct entries help prevent incorrect decisions and actions.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect unit/sign/decimal in a parameter entry is a recording defect that can drive dangerous misdosing (relevance 7); magnitude errors can be catastrophic (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Input validation catches many unit and sign errors, but free-form or misconfigured fields still let some through. Format validation catches unit and decimal errors at entry rather than at review. Automated range and unit checks reduce reliance on operator precision. Validation masks are configured once within field definitions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of production batches executed with obsolete recipe revisions
rows.plain: Fewer production batches should be made using an outdated version of the recipe. Using the current recipe helps keep production consistent and within required rules.
rows.imp_rat: Executing with obsolete revisions directly violates recipe enforcement (relevance 8) and produces batches against outdated, possibly unsafe specs (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Recipe lifecycle management with effective-dating is mature, though interfaced or paper-parallel systems occasionally allow obsolete revisions through. Effective-revision enforcement at batch start prevents obsolete downloads instantly. Automatic serving of the current effective revision removes operator judgement about which version to use. Avoiding obsolete-revision batches prevents rejected product and rework spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the approved recipe revision or parameter set is unavailable at scheduled batch start
rows.plain: Have the approved instructions and settings ready when the batch is scheduled to start. This helps prevent delays and ensures the batch uses the correct process.
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable approved recipe at batch start blocks the core execute job (relevance 8); it mainly delays or risks improvised start (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Recipe management systems keep approved revisions accessible, though approval-workflow lags and library sync gaps occasionally leave a revision missing at start. Approved recipes are normally pre-loaded before the shift so availability at start is usually immediate. Retrieving the right approved revision requires little expertise once the recipe library is maintained. An unavailable recipe delays a batch window and ties up staff, but this is infrequent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterile-compounding and contamination-control constraints omitted from fit criteria
rows.plain: Fewer requirements for making products free from contamination and for working with sterile materials should be left out when judging whether an approach is suitable. Including them helps ensure the approach can be used safely.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting contamination-control constraints from fit criteria is a design-completeness concern outside live execution (relevance 5) but misses controls with severe safety stakes (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Contamination-control constraints are frequently under-represented in generic fit checklists, so critical constraints are omitted and inconsistently caught. Sterile and contamination-control constraints are numerous and cross-referenced, and enumerating them fully exceeds the assessment window. It takes rare combined aseptic-processing and control-system expertise to translate contamination-control constraints into fit criteria. A missed sterility constraint can invalidate batches, making omissions extremely expensive relative to review cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recorded addition quantities with a missing or incorrect unit of measure
rows.plain: Record the amount of each added material with the correct unit, such as kilograms or liters. This prevents confusion about how much was used.
rows.imp_rat: Missing/incorrect unit on a recorded addition quantity is a recording defect (relevance 7) that can misrepresent dose with serious potency implications (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: System-enforced units make missing or wrong UOM rare where master data is complete. Pre-defined units on dispense fields avoid delay from correcting them later. Fixed unit-of-measure fields relieve operators from specifying units correctly. Unit definitions are set in material and recipe master data.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a deviation indication fails to reach the operator during the departure
rows.plain: Make sure the operator is warned when the work starts to go off course. This lets the operator correct the problem during the departure process.
rows.imp_rat: A deviation indication failing to reach the operator blocks timely enforcement response (relevance 7); an unseen departure can persist and harm the batch (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: SCADA alarming reliably reaches operators for configured points, but unconfigured or suppressed alarms can still fail to notify. Alarms annunciate in real time so notification is prompt when configured. Alarm routing reduces reliance on the operator constantly scanning for departures. A missed alarm can cost a batch, partly guarded by redundant annunciation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of captured entries lost or overwritten during compounding
rows.plain: Reduce the number of recorded entries that disappear or are replaced while the product is being mixed. This preserves a complete and trustworthy record of the work.
rows.imp_rat: Lost or overwritten captured entries directly breaks the recording job (relevance 7) and is a serious data-integrity/GMP violation (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Database commit and audit trails prevent most loss or overwrite, though buffering gaps and concurrent edits occasionally lose entries. Transactional databases commit entries continuously so little is lost to timing. Persistence is handled by the system, requiring no operator effort. Reliable storage is a built-in feature of validated systems.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between recorded event chronology and actual execution chronology
rows.plain: Keep the order and timing recorded in the records aligned with what actually happened during the work. This helps people trust the records and reconstruct the work accurately.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between recorded and actual event chronology is a data-integrity defect in recording (relevance 7) with serious regulatory implications (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Electronic chronology is generally faithful, though asynchronous manual entries can distort actual order. System clocks timestamp events as they occur, preserving chronology automatically. Automated timestamping removes reliance on operators to note event order. Timestamping uses existing system clocks at no added cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of affected batches with incomplete recipe and process-parameter records
rows.plain: Complete the records showing the recipe and operating settings for every affected production batch. This gives investigators the information needed to understand what happened.
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete recipe/parameter records fail the recording pillar (relevance 7); incomplete records can block release and violate GMP (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: EBR/MES capture most parameters reliably, but manual entries, instrument gaps and interface failures still leave some records incomplete. Electronic batch records capture parameters automatically as the batch runs, avoiding after-the-fact assembly delays. Automated data capture reduces reliance on staff to manually complete records. Incomplete records force costly reconstruction and can render batches unreleasable.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a brief parameter excursion occurs between captured observations during recipe execution
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that a process setting briefly goes outside its allowed range between recorded checks. This matters because a short problem could be missed.
rows.imp_rat: A brief excursion between observations escapes capture (relevance 6); such gaps can hide out-of-limit conditions affecting quality (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Where sampling is periodic rather than continuous, short excursions genuinely slip between observations, a real gap. Brief excursions between samples pass in seconds, faster than periodic observation intervals catch. Catching transient excursions demands continuous vigilance no human can sustain between captures. An unseen transient can compromise a batch, creating latent cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of production batches initiated before required recipe approvals are complete
rows.plain: Prevent production batches from starting until all required instructions have been reviewed and approved. This helps ensure production follows accepted and permitted methods.
rows.imp_rat: Starting before approvals complete is a control-gate failure (relevance 7); it risks unvalidated production and compliance violation (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Enforced release-to-production gates are common, but production pressure and manual overrides still let some batches begin before approvals are fully complete. Approval-status checks at batch start are automated and quick where the system enforces gating. System gating removes reliance on a supervisor manually verifying approval completeness. Preventing premature starts avoids wasted material and re-execution costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of active recipe revisions lacking required validation or change-control evidence
rows.plain: Reduce the number of current production instructions that have not been properly tested or supported by records showing that changes were reviewed. This helps ensure production follows approved and reliable instructions.
rows.imp_rat: Active revisions lacking validation/change-control evidence is a governance gap somewhat aside from execution (relevance 6) but a serious compliance and quality risk (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Change-control systems track status but active revisions lacking complete validation evidence still slip through where linkage is not enforced. Linking validation evidence to revisions is a manual, sometimes slow curation task. Confirming adequacy of validation and change-control evidence relies on quality specialists' judgement. Missing evidence triggers costly remediation and re-validation efforts.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required sequences, parameter limits, and interlocks that the recommended approach cannot enforce
rows.plain: Make sure the recommended approach can require the right order of steps, keep settings within allowed limits, and prevent unsafe actions. This shows whether the approach can actually control the work as needed.
rows.imp_rat: An approach unable to enforce required sequences/interlocks undermines the enforcement mandate (relevance 6); unenforced controls permit unsafe processing (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Interlock and parameter-limit enforcement is sometimes demonstrated but seldom exhaustively verified, leaving unenforceable cases unknown at recommendation time. Verifying every sequence, limit, and interlock takes far longer than the evaluation schedule permits, so coverage is partial. Judging whether a candidate can truly enforce interlocks requires automation-engineering skill that the influencer often must borrow. Unenforceable interlocks discovered post-purchase drive rework and custom development spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of misidentifying the root cause of a compounding disruption
rows.plain: Identify the true underlying cause of a problem that interrupts mixing or preparation, rather than blaming the wrong cause. This helps the team fix the problem effectively and prevent it from happening again.
rows.imp_rat: Misidentifying disruption root cause is investigative, downstream of execution (relevance 5); wrong cause allows recurrence and possible bad release (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: With fragmented data and thin real-time diagnostics, the status quo frequently produces a wrong or incomplete root cause, making this a chronic pain. Root-cause identification typically drags out well beyond the shift, delaying corrective action. Correctly diagnosing a compounding disruption relies on scarce senior engineering and process expertise rarely present on every shift. Misdiagnosis leads to repeated failures and wasted troubleshooting labor and material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical compounding scenarios lacking sufficient enforcement evidence
rows.plain: Have fewer important mixing situations without enough proof that the required rules were followed. This helps show that each mixing process meets its obligations.
rows.imp_rat: Critical scenarios lacking enforcement evidence is a validation-completeness concern (relevance 5); unproven enforcement can fail in production (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Recommendations commonly rest on demonstrations of a few happy-path scenarios, leaving most critical scenarios with insufficient enforcement evidence. Gathering enforcement evidence for every critical scenario is time-intensive and usually truncated before completion. Knowing what constitutes sufficient enforcement evidence for a scenario requires validation expertise not consistently on hand. Thin evidence leads to costly rework when unproven scenarios fail in qualification.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between occurrence of an execution departure and operator recognition
rows.plain: Shorten the time between a process mistake happening and the operator noticing it. Faster awareness allows the problem to be corrected sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Time between departure occurrence and operator recognition governs correction speed (relevance 7); slow recognition prolongs harm (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Recognition latency is low for alarmed parameters but longer for conditions relying on manual observation. Real-time alarms shorten the gap between departure and operator recognition substantially. Automated detection reduces the vigilance operators must maintain to notice departures. Faster recognition limits the material lost during a departure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recipe steps confirmed with required parameter records missing
rows.plain: Reduce the number of process steps marked as finished when required records of their settings or results are missing. This helps ensure completion is supported by the needed evidence.
rows.imp_rat: Steps confirmed with missing parameter records fails recording completeness (relevance 7); gaps impede release evidence (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Where parameter-to-step gating is configured it works well, but not all steps are consistently interlocked. Systems can block step confirmation until required parameters are entered, avoiding later rework. Gating logic offloads the check that all parameters accompany a step from the operator. Linking parameters to step confirmations is a configuration effort within the recipe.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that adjusting one critical parameter drives another beyond specified limits
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that changing one important setting pushes another setting outside its allowed range. This helps keep the process stable and safe.
rows.imp_rat: One adjustment driving another parameter out of limits is a coupling-stability issue in enforcement (relevance 6) that can create hidden excursions (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Most control is single-variable, so multivariable interactions are inconsistently managed and remain a real risk. Interacting-parameter effects appear in real time but are hard to anticipate quickly. Understanding how adjusting one parameter drives another requires significant process expertise. Cross-coupled excursions can ruin batches, poorly guarded by single-loop control.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required critical-parameter observations missing during the compounding run
rows.plain: Reduce the share of required checks of important process conditions that are missing during a mixing run. This helps show whether the process stayed under control from start to finish.
rows.imp_rat: Missing required critical-parameter observations weakens both recording and enforcement (relevance 7); gaps undermine release evidence (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Critical observations are largely enforced, though manual critical checks can still slip during high-tempo operations. Real-time monitoring flags missing critical observations during the run rather than afterward. Automated critical-parameter prompts reduce dependence on operator vigilance for the most important readings. Critical-parameter monitoring rides on existing instrumentation and control layers.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of execution departures that end before detection
rows.plain: Reduce the number of times work goes off course and finishes before anyone notices. This helps people find and correct mistakes before the work is completed.
rows.imp_rat: Departures that end before detection escape enforcement and record (relevance 6); undetected excursions can compromise the batch unnoticed (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: High-speed historians capture some transients, but sampling gaps mean brief departures still end undetected. Short-lived departures may end before the scan or operator notices them. Catching transient departures depends on operator attentiveness and data resolution. Undetected transients can still affect quality, an underaddressed cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unavailable required measurements during endpoint determination
rows.plain: Shorten the time that necessary measurements are unavailable when deciding whether a process has reached its required endpoint. This helps workers make timely and reliable decisions about when to stop.
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable measurements during endpoint determination blocks correct endpoint enforcement (relevance 6); blind endpoint calls can under/over-process (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Sensor availability is generally good but gaps in required measurements still stall endpoint decisions. Waiting for unavailable measurements during endpoint determination causes real delay. Working around missing measurements requires operator improvisation. Delays idle resources and risk product, partly controlled.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of material compounding-record discrepancies omitted from evaluation
rows.plain: Reduce the share of differences in material records that are left out of the review. This helps ensure record mistakes or conflicts are properly considered before decisions are made.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting record discrepancies from evaluation is a review-completeness gap peripheral to executing recipes (relevance 5); undetected issues carry moderate-high risk (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: MES exception reports help but discrepancies buried in free-text or paper records are routinely missed. Manually reconciling compounding records to surface every material discrepancy is a lengthy line-by-line effort. Judging which discrepancies are material requires trained review judgement that is inconsistently available. Overlooked discrepancies cause expensive downstream failures while thorough review consumes reviewer hours.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required parameter or recipe-step entries missing from the compounding record
rows.plain: Record every required setting and mixing step in the product record. Complete records make it possible to confirm exactly how the product was made.
rows.imp_rat: Missing required parameter/step entries from the record fails the recording pillar (relevance 7) and blocks defensible release (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Mandatory entry logic makes missing required entries rare where the recipe is well built. Entry-time validation prevents record gaps from propagating to later stages. Field-level enforcement lessens the operator's memory load for required entries. Validation rules are configured once and reused across batches.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required process parameters missing from the compounding batch record
rows.plain: Make sure the production record includes every process setting that must be recorded while materials are being combined. This provides a complete record of how the product was made.
rows.imp_rat: Required parameters missing from the batch record fails the recording job (relevance 7); gaps impede release and reconstruction (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Electronic records enforce completeness well, so missing required parameters are uncommon where the recipe is properly configured. Mandatory-field enforcement catches omissions at entry time rather than at review. Required-field logic offloads the burden of remembering every parameter from the operator. Configuring required fields is a one-time engineering cost in the recipe build.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable data-integrity and regulatory expectations omitted from evaluation
rows.plain: Make sure the evaluation includes all relevant rules for keeping records accurate and meeting government requirements. This prevents important compliance checks from being left out.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting regulatory expectations from evaluation is an assessment-completeness concern peripheral to real-time execution (relevance 5) but carries compliance risk (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Requirement checklists exist but are frequently incomplete or outdated, so omissions recur across evaluations. Assembling the full set of applicable expectations for each evaluation is slow and often done under deadline pressure. Knowing which regulatory expectations actually apply demands scarce regulatory expertise that varies between reviewers. Missed expectations trigger costly rework and findings, and maintaining current requirement libraries is a standing overhead.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of prescribed recipe steps absent from the completed execution record
rows.plain: Make sure the final work record includes all the steps that were supposed to be followed for making the product. This helps show that the work was completed correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Prescribed steps absent from execution record is a recording-completeness defect (relevance 7) that undermines traceability and release (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Enforced step gating makes absent steps in the record uncommon in a properly configured EBR. Sequential electronic workflows record each executed step as it is completed. Guided step-by-step execution removes reliance on operators recalling which steps to log. Step sequencing is part of the standard recipe configuration cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of active compounding intervals with unavailable execution-condition data
rows.plain: Make sure information about the conditions during each active mixing period is available. This helps identify when the process may not have run as expected.
rows.imp_rat: Intervals with unavailable execution-condition data leave gaps in both recording and enforcement (relevance 6) that can hide unsafe conditions (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Historian and SCADA availability is high, but network or sensor gaps still leave intervals without condition data. Data-unavailable intervals interrupt smooth execution timing. Operators must compensate manually when execution-condition data drops out. Data gaps risk uncontrolled processing, a partly contained cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which exception, deviation, and recovery needs are inadequately evaluated
rows.plain: Thoroughly evaluate what is needed when something unusual happens, work falls outside the plan, or the process must recover. This helps ensure the proposed approach can handle problems safely and effectively.
rows.imp_rat: Inadequate evaluation of exception/recovery needs is a design-readiness concern peripheral to execution (relevance 5) with moderate impact when disruptions occur (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Exception and recovery handling is chronically under-evaluated because demos emphasize normal flow, so these needs are inadequately assessed. Exception, deviation, and recovery paths are the last thing examined and usually run out of evaluation time. Evaluating recovery behavior demands hard-won operational and control-recovery expertise that is scarce. Poorly evaluated recovery needs cause expensive downtime and batch loss in production.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of missing or stale critical parameter readings during production
rows.plain: Shorten the time that important readings are missing or no longer current during production. This helps workers notice and respond to problems sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Missing/stale critical readings during production blinds enforcement (relevance 6); operating blind can let excursions go uncontrolled (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Redundant instrumentation limits data gaps, but sensor faults still produce stale readings occasionally. Stale or missing readings introduce decision delays during production. Judging around stale data requires operator experience. Missing data can force conservative overprocessing, a modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical recipe-control and record-integrity requirements omitted from acceptance criteria
rows.plain: Include all important recipe controls and record-accuracy requirements in the pass-or-fail rules. This helps ensure the system works correctly and keeps trustworthy records.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting critical requirements from acceptance criteria is an evaluation-completeness gap peripheral to execution (relevance 5) with real compliance risk (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Requirement-to-criteria mapping tools help, but critical items still get dropped when specifications are ambiguous or evolving. Deriving acceptance criteria from requirements is a time-intensive upfront activity often compressed late in the project. Knowing which recipe-control and record-integrity requirements are critical requires deep domain and regulatory judgement not all reviewers possess. Omissions are cheap to make and expensive to discover later, and thorough criteria development is under-resourced.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that recipe-step and process-parameter enforcement gaps remain undetected during fit assessment
rows.plain: Make sure testing reveals any missing controls that should enforce recipe steps and operating settings. This helps show whether the proposed approach can reliably control the process.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected enforcement gaps during fit assessment is a design-evaluation concern peripheral to live execution (relevance 5) with moderate downstream risk (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Fit assessment relies on vendor assertions and slide decks rather than adversarial testing, so enforcement gaps very commonly remain undetected. Detecting hidden enforcement gaps within the short fit-assessment window is rarely achievable without lengthy hands-on probing that timelines don't allow. Spotting a latent enforcement gap requires deep control-logic knowledge that most evaluators lack, so gaps stay invisible. Undetected gaps become costly change orders once the system is deployed against real recipes.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a critical parameter adjustment crosses the opposite specified limit
rows.plain: When changing an important setting, keep it from moving past the allowed limit in the other direction. This helps keep the process safe and working as intended.
